function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
SpaceX intends to rely on Nasdaq controlled-company exemptions following the IPO. Identify precisely which board-level and committee-level independence requirements are waived under those exemptions, which requirements remain fully applicable regardless of controlled-company status, and whether any waiver is conditioned on SpaceX maintaining controlled-company status continuously. Where does the filing disclose the specific exemptions being relied upon?

steps.reasoning_content: The user is asking about SpaceX's controlled-company exemptions under Nasdaq rules. I need to find the relevant disclosure in the S-1 filing. Let me parse the filing and search for the controlled-company exemption language.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

arguments.prompt: Find the disclosure about controlled company exemptions under Nasdaq rules in {{spacex_s1}}. Specifically, identify: (1) which board-level and committee-level independence requirements are waived under the controlled company exemptions, (2) which requirements remain fully applicable regardless of controlled company status, (3) whether any waiver is conditioned on SpaceX maintaining controlled company status continuously, and (4) where in the filing the specific exemptions being relied upon are disclosed.

results.content:
# Controlled Company Exemptions Under Nasdaq Rules

## (1) Board-Level and Committee-Level Independence Requirements Waived

The filing discloses four specific Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas corporate governance requirements that the company may elect not to comply with as a controlled company:

**Board-level:**
- The requirement that **a majority of the board of directors consist of independent directors** as defined under the listing rules.

**Committee-level (Nominating):**
- The requirement that **director nominees be selected or recommended by a nominating committee composed entirely of independent directors**, with a written charter addressing the nominations process.

**Committee-level (Compensation):**
- The requirement that **the compensation committee be composed entirely of independent directors** with a written charter addressing the committee's purpose and responsibilities.

**Committee-level (Evaluations):**
- The requirement that **annual performance evaluations of the compensation and nominating committees be conducted.**

The company explicitly states its intent to rely on these exemptions:

> "Following the completion of this offering, we intend to utilize certain of these exemptions. As a result, we do not expect to have a compensation and nominating committee that is composed entirely of independent directors or that has a committee charter that addresses all Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas requirements applicable to companies that are not controlled companies."

The compensation and nominating committee's expected members are **Ira Ehrenpreis, Antonio J. Gracias, and Luke Nosek**—and the filing notes that Gracias does **not** qualify as independent, confirming the reliance on the exemption. By contrast, only Ehrenpreis and Nosek are identified as satisfying the heightened independence standards for compensation committee members.

---

## (2) Requirements That Remain Fully Applicable

Despite controlled company status, certain requirements remain in force:

- **Audit committee independence:** The filing states: *"As a controlled company, we will remain subject to rules that require us to have an audit committee composed entirely of independent directors."* The expected audit committee members, **Randy Glein and Steve Jurvetson**, each qualify as independent under **Rule 10A-3 of the Exchange Act** and the Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas listing standards. The company also commits to identifying a third audit committee member within the applicable one-year transition period.

- **Audit committee financial expert requirement:** Randy Glein is expected to qualify as an "audit committee financial expert" under SEC rules implementing Section 407 of Sarbanes-Oxley.

- **Code of business conduct and ethics:** The board will adopt a code compliant with applicable SEC rules and Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas corporate governance rules.

- **Corporate governance guidelines:** The company intends to adopt governance guidelines setting forth director expectations, qualification standards, committee structure, and functions.

- **Nasdaq shareholder approval requirements for certain issuances:** The filing notes that issuances equal to or exceeding 20% of then-outstanding voting power or shares require shareholder approval regardless of controlled company status.

---

## (3) Conditioning on Continuous Controlled Company Status

**Yes—the exemptions are expressly conditioned on SpaceX maintaining controlled company status.** The filing states this in two key passages:

> "Additionally, we may elect to take advantage of certain other exemptions in the future **for as long as we remain a 'controlled company.'**"

> "In the event that we cease to be a 'controlled company' and our shares continue to be listed on Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas, **we will be required to comply with all of the applicable governance requirements within the applicable transition periods.**"

The controlled company status itself is tied to Mr. Musk's beneficial ownership of greater than 50% of the voting power. The Class B common stock—held predominantly by Musk—elects 51% of the board. If Musk's voting power were to fall below 50%, the company would lose controlled company status and would need to cure any non-compliance within the applicable Nasdaq transition periods.

---

## (4) Locations in the Filing Where the Exemptions Are Disclosed

The specific exemptions being relied upon are disclosed in **multiple locations** throughout the prospectus:

| Location | Description |
|---|---|
| **Prospectus summary table (cover/preamble)** | Under the "Controlled company" line item, directing readers to "Management—Controlled Company Exemption" and "Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions." |
| **Risk Factors section** | Discloses that following the offering, the company "will be a 'controlled company' within the meaning of the Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules and, as a result, will qualify for and rely on exemptions from certain [requirements]." Lists all four waived requirements. |
| **Management—Corporate Governance section (Controlled Company Exemption)** | Provides the most detailed disclosure: defines controlled company, enumerates all four exempted requirements, states intent to utilize exemptions, warns shareholders of reduced protections, and addresses transition obligations if status is lost. |
| **Management—Corporate Governance section (Compensation and Nominating Committee)** | Specifically discloses reliance on the exemption from the independent-director and charter requirements for the compensation and nominating committee, identifying the expected committee members and their independence qualifications. |
| **Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions section** | Notes Mr. Musk's post-IPO ownership of approximately [ ]% of Class B common stock and [ ]% of total voting power, establishing the factual basis for controlled company status. |

The most comprehensive single disclosure of the specific exemptions appears in the **Corporate Governance section** under the controlled company exemption discussion, where the filing lists each waived requirement by bullet point and then states the company's intent to rely on them.

steps.message: Let me verify the specific language and details by retrieving more targeted information from the filing.
steps.reasoning_content: I have a comprehensive answer from the retrieval. Let me verify some of the key details by retrieving more specific information to ensure accuracy, particularly around the audit committee requirements and the specific language used in the filing.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, find the exact verbatim language from the Risk Factors section and the Management—Corporate Governance section discussing the controlled company exemptions. I need the precise wording about: (1) the four specific exemptions being relied upon, (2) the audit committee requirement that remains applicable, (3) the language about "for as long as we remain a controlled company" and the transition period if status is lost, and (4) the definition of controlled company under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas rules.

results.content:
Based on the provided text chunks, here is the exact verbatim language regarding the controlled company exemptions:

**1. The four specific exemptions being relied upon:**
"certain Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas corporate governance requirements, including the requirements that:
• a majority of such company’s board of directors consist of independent directors as defined under the listing
• director nominees be selected or recommended for board of directors’ selection by a nominating committee
composed entirely of independent directors, with a written charter addressing the nominations process as
required under the listing rules of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas;
• the compensation committee be composed entirely of independent directors with a written charter addressing
the committee’s purpose and responsibilities; and
• annual performance evaluations of the compensation and nominating committees be conducted."

**2. The audit committee requirement that remains applicable:**
"A controlled company is not required to have a majority of its board composed of independent directors or to establish independent compensation and nominating committees. As a controlled company, we will remain subject to rules that require us to have an audit committee composed entirely of independent directors."

**3. The language about "for as long as we remain a controlled company" and the transition period if status is lost:**
"Additionally, we may elect to take advantage of certain other exemptions in the future for as long as we remain a “controlled company.” Accordingly, our Class A shareholders will not have the same protections afforded to shareholders of companies that are subject to all of the corporate governance requirements of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas. In the event that we cease to be a “controlled company” and our shares continue to be listed on Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas, we will be required to comply with all of the applicable governance requirements within the applicable transition periods."

**4. The definition of controlled company under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas rules:**
"Under the listing rules of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas, a company of which more than 50% of the voting power with respect to director elections is held by another person or group of persons acting together is a “controlled company” and may elect not to comply with certain Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas corporate governance requirements..."

arguments.final_result:
## SpaceX's Reliance on Nasdaq Controlled-Company Exemptions

### 1. Board-Level and Committee-Level Independence Requirements WAIVED

The S-1 discloses that, as a "controlled company" under the listing rules of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas, SpaceX may elect not to comply with four specific corporate governance requirements. The filing enumerates them verbatim:

1. **Board-level (majority-independent board):** The requirement that **a majority of the board of directors consist of independent directors** as defined under the listing rules.

2. **Committee-level (Nominating committee):** The requirement that **director nominees be selected or recommended for the board's selection by a nominating committee composed entirely of independent directors**, with a written charter addressing the nominations process as required under the Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules.

3. **Committee-level (Compensation committee):** The requirement that **the compensation committee be composed entirely of independent directors** with a written charter addressing the committee's purpose and responsibilities.

4. **Committee-level (Annual evaluations):** The requirement that **annual performance evaluations of the compensation and nominating committees be conducted.**

SpaceX expressly states its intent to rely on these exemptions:

> "Following the completion of this offering, we intend to utilize certain of these exemptions. As a result, we do not expect to have a compensation and nominating committee that is composed entirely of independent directors or that has a committee charter that addresses all Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas requirements applicable to companies that are not controlled companies."

The expected members of the compensation and nominating committee are Ira Ehrenpreis, Antonio J. Gracias, and Luke Nosek — and the filing notes that Gracias does not qualify as independent, confirming reliance on the exemption. Only Ehrenpreis and Nosek satisfy the heightened independence standards for compensation committee members.

### 2. Requirements That REMAIN Fully Applicable Regardless of Controlled-Company Status

Despite controlled-company status, the following requirements remain in force:

- **Audit committee independence (Rule 10A-3 / Nasdaq):** The filing states: *"As a controlled company, we will remain subject to rules that require us to have an audit committee composed entirely of independent directors."* The expected audit committee members, Randy Glein and Steve Jurvetson, each qualify as independent under Rule 10A-3 of the Exchange Act and the Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas listing standards. SpaceX commits to identifying a third audit committee member within the applicable one-year transition period.

- **Audit committee financial expert requirement:** Randy Glein is expected to qualify as an "audit committee financial expert" under SEC rules implementing Section 407 of Sarbanes-Oxley.

- **Code of business conduct and ethics:** The board will adopt a code compliant with applicable SEC rules and Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas corporate governance rules.

- **Corporate governance guidelines:** SpaceX intends to adopt governance guidelines setting forth director expectations, qualification standards, committee structure, and functions.

- **Nasdaq shareholder approval requirements for certain issuances:** Issuances equal to or exceeding 20% of then-outstanding voting power or shares require shareholder approval regardless of controlled-company status.

### 3. Conditioning on Continuous Controlled-Company Status

**Yes — the exemptions are expressly conditioned on SpaceX maintaining controlled-company status.** The filing states this in two key passages:

> "Additionally, we may elect to take advantage of certain other exemptions in the future **for as long as we remain a 'controlled company.'**"

> "In the event that we cease to be a 'controlled company' and our shares continue to be listed on Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas, **we will be required to comply with all of the applicable governance requirements within the applicable transition periods.**"

Controlled-company status itself is tied to Mr. Musk's beneficial ownership of greater than 50% of the voting power for director elections. The Class B common stock — held predominantly by Musk — elects 51% of the board. If Musk's voting power were to fall below 50%, SpaceX would lose controlled-company status and would be required to cure any non-compliance within the applicable Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas transition periods.

The filing defines a controlled company as follows:

> "Under the listing rules of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas, a company of which more than 50% of the voting power with respect to director elections is held by another person or group of persons acting together is a 'controlled company' and may elect not to comply with certain Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas corporate governance requirements..."

### 4. Where the Filing Discloses the Specific Exemptions Being Relied Upon

The specific exemptions are disclosed in multiple locations throughout the prospectus:

| Location | Description |
|---|---|
| **Prospectus summary table** | Under the "Controlled company" line item, directing readers to "Management—Controlled Company Exemption" and "Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions." |
| **Risk Factors section** | Discloses that following the offering, SpaceX "will be a 'controlled company' within the meaning of the Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules and, as a result, will qualify for and rely on exemptions from certain [requirements]." Lists all four waived requirements. |
| **Management—Corporate Governance section (Controlled Company Exemption)** | Provides the most detailed disclosure: defines controlled company, enumerates all four exempted requirements, states intent to utilize exemptions, warns shareholders of reduced protections, and addresses transition obligations if status is lost. |
| **Management—Corporate Governance section (Compensation and Nominating Committee)** | Specifically discloses reliance on the exemption from the independent-director and charter requirements for the compensation and nominating committee, identifying the expected committee members and their independence qualifications. |
| **Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions section** | Notes Mr. Musk's post-IPO ownership of Class B common stock and total voting power, establishing the factual basis for controlled-company status. |

The most comprehensive single disclosure of the specific exemptions appears in the **Corporate Governance section** under the controlled company exemption discussion, where the filing lists each waived requirement by bullet point and then states SpaceX's intent to rely on them.

---

### Summary

SpaceX will rely on four Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas controlled-company exemptions: (1) majority-independent board, (2) independent nominating committee with charter, (3) independent compensation committee with charter, and (4) annual performance evaluations of the compensation and nominating committees. The audit committee independence requirement (Rule 10A-3), the audit committee financial expert requirement, the code of business conduct and ethics, corporate governance guidelines, and the 20% shareholder-approval rule all remain fully applicable. The exemptions are expressly conditioned on SpaceX remaining a controlled company — if it ceases to be one, it must comply with all applicable governance requirements within the applicable Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas transition periods. The specific exemptions are disclosed primarily in the Risk Factors section and the Management—Corporate Governance section (under "Controlled Company Exemption"), with cross-references in the prospectus summary and Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions sections.

      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Risk Factors; Management—Corporate Governance (Controlled Company Exemption); Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions"
    }
  ]

### 1. Board-Level and Committee-Level Independence Requirements WAIVED

The S-1 discloses that, as a "controlled company" under the listing rules of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas, SpaceX may elect not to comply with four specific corporate governance requirements. The filing enumerates them verbatim:

1. **Board-level (majority-independent board):** The requirement that **a majority of the board of directors consist of independent directors** as defined under the listing rules.

2. **Committee-level (Nominating committee):** The requirement that **director nominees be selected or recommended for the board's selection by a nominating committee composed entirely of independent directors**, with a written charter addressing the nominations process as required under the Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules.

3. **Committee-level (Compensation committee):** The requirement that **the compensation committee be composed entirely of independent directors** with a written charter addressing the committee's purpose and responsibilities.

4. **Committee-level (Annual evaluations):** The requirement that **annual performance evaluations of the compensation and nominating committees be conducted.**

SpaceX expressly states its intent to rely on these exemptions:

> "Following the completion of this offering, we intend to utilize certain of these exemptions. As a result, we do not expect to have a compensation and nominating committee that is composed entirely of independent directors or that has a committee charter that addresses all Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas requirements applicable to companies that are not controlled companies."

The expected members of the compensation and nominating committee are Ira Ehrenpreis, Antonio J. Gracias, and Luke Nosek — and the filing notes that Gracias does not qualify as independent, confirming reliance on the exemption. Only Ehrenpreis and Nosek satisfy the heightened independence standards for compensation committee members.

### 2. Requirements That REMAIN Fully Applicable Regardless of Controlled-Company Status

Despite controlled-company status, the following requirements remain in force:

- **Audit committee independence (Rule 10A-3 / Nasdaq):** The filing states: *"As a controlled company, we will remain subject to rules that require us to have an audit committee composed entirely of independent directors."* The expected audit committee members, Randy Glein and Steve Jurvetson, each qualify as independent under Rule 10A-3 of the Exchange Act and the Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas listing standards. SpaceX commits to identifying a third audit committee member within the applicable one-year transition period.

- **Audit committee financial expert requirement:** Randy Glein is expected to qualify as an "audit committee financial expert" under SEC rules implementing Section 407 of Sarbanes-Oxley.

- **Code of business conduct and ethics:** The board will adopt a code compliant with applicable SEC rules and Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas corporate governance rules.

- **Corporate governance guidelines:** SpaceX intends to adopt governance guidelines setting forth director expectations, qualification standards, committee structure, and functions.

- **Nasdaq shareholder approval requirements for certain issuances:** Issuances equal to or exceeding 20% of then-outstanding voting power or shares require shareholder approval regardless of controlled-company status.

### 3. Conditioning on Continuous Controlled-Company Status

**Yes — the exemptions are expressly conditioned on SpaceX maintaining controlled-company status.** The filing states this in two key passages:

> "Additionally, we may elect to take advantage of certain other exemptions in the future **for as long as we remain a 'controlled company.'**"

> "In the event that we cease to be a 'controlled company' and our shares continue to be listed on Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas, **we will be required to comply with all of the applicable governance requirements within the applicable transition periods.**"

Controlled-company status itself is tied to Mr. Musk's beneficial ownership of greater than 50% of the voting power for director elections. The Class B common stock — held predominantly by Musk — elects 51% of the board. If Musk's voting power were to fall below 50%, SpaceX would lose controlled-company status and would be required to cure any non-compliance within the applicable Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas transition periods.

The filing defines a controlled company as follows:

> "Under the listing rules of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas, a company of which more than 50% of the voting power with respect to director elections is held by another person or group of persons acting together is a 'controlled company' and may elect not to comply with certain Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas corporate governance requirements..."

### 4. Where the Filing Discloses the Specific Exemptions Being Relied Upon

The specific exemptions are disclosed in multiple locations throughout the prospectus:

| Location | Description |
|---|---|
| **Prospectus summary table** | Under the "Controlled company" line item, directing readers to "Management—Controlled Company Exemption" and "Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions." |
| **Risk Factors section** | Discloses that following the offering, SpaceX "will be a 'controlled company' within the meaning of the Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules and, as a result, will qualify for and rely on exemptions from certain [requirements]." Lists all four waived requirements. |
| **Management—Corporate Governance section (Controlled Company Exemption)** | Provides the most detailed disclosure: defines controlled company, enumerates all four exempted requirements, states intent to utilize exemptions, warns shareholders of reduced protections, and addresses transition obligations if status is lost. |
| **Management—Corporate Governance section (Compensation and Nominating Committee)** | Specifically discloses reliance on the exemption from the independent-director and charter requirements for the compensation and nominating committee, identifying the expected committee members and their independence qualifications. |
| **Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions section** | Notes Mr. Musk's post-IPO ownership of Class B common stock and total voting power, establishing the factual basis for controlled-company status. |

The most comprehensive single disclosure of the specific exemptions appears in the **Corporate Governance section** under the controlled company exemption discussion, where the filing lists each waived requirement by bullet point and then states SpaceX's intent to rely on them.

---

### Summary

SpaceX will rely on four Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas controlled-company exemptions: (1) majority-independent board, (2) independent nominating committee with charter, (3) independent compensation committee with charter, and (4) annual performance evaluations of the compensation and nominating committees. The audit committee independence requirement (Rule 10A-3), the audit committee financial expert requirement, the code of business conduct and ethics, corporate governance guidelines, and the 20% shareholder-approval rule all remain fully applicable. The exemptions are expressly conditioned on SpaceX remaining a controlled company — if it ceases to be one, it must comply with all applicable governance requirements within the applicable Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas transition periods. The specific exemptions are disclosed primarily in the Risk Factors section and the Management—Corporate Governance section (under "Controlled Company Exemption"), with cross-references in the prospectus summary and Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions sections.
